title: Therapeutic Hypothermia for Neurological Injuries: Balancing Neuroprotection with Risks.

title: Therapeutic Hypothermia for Neurological Injuries: Balancing Neuroprotection with Risks.
chunk_text: This review introduces the history of hypothermia in medicine first, followed by a description of cellular mechanisms behind its neuroprotective effects observed in animal studies and some clinical studies.

title: Therapeutic Hypothermia for Neurological Injuries: Balancing Neuroprotection with Risks.

title: Therapeutic Hypothermia for Neurological Injuries: Balancing Neuroprotection with Risks.

title: Therapeutic Hypothermia for Neurological Injuries: Balancing Neuroprotection with Risks.

title: Therapeutic Hypothermia for Neurological Injuries: Balancing Neuroprotection with Risks.
chunk_text: The review also examines the risks and benefits of hypothermia, supported or disputed by clinical studies.

title: Therapeutic Hypothermia for Neurological Injuries: Balancing Neuroprotection with Risks.
chunk_text: Contributions from bioengineers in the research field are presented in the last section, with details of cooling device design and theoretical simulations.

title: Therapeutic Hypothermia for Neurological Injuries: Balancing Neuroprotection with Risks.
chunk_text: Ultimately, the review highlights that successful hypothermia treatment hinges on achieving targeted tissue cooling quickly after injury, with mild hypothermia often being proven as effective as deeper cooling, provided a slow rewarming rate is implemented.

title: Missed opportunities to promote flourishing in cancer care: a brief examination of multiple myeloma.

title: Missed opportunities to promote flourishing in cancer care: a brief examination of multiple myeloma.

title: Caregiver decision-making on pediatric research participation in congenital heart disease in western China: a qualitative study.

title: Calcium and TRPML-Mediated Autophagy: Implications in Cancer, Cardiovascular Diseases, and Cardio-Oncology.
chunk_text: Autophagy is an essential cellular process that maintains homeostasis, regulates organelle turnover, preserves energy balance, and ensures protein quality control.

title: Calcium and TRPML-Mediated Autophagy: Implications in Cancer, Cardiovascular Diseases, and Cardio-Oncology.
chunk_text: Central to autophagy regulation is calcium (Ca²⁺) signaling, which integrates inputs from multiple Ca²⁺ channels and handling proteins, including L-type and T-type voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels, transient receptor potential mucolipin (TRPML) channels, inositol 1,4,5-trisphosphate receptors (IP3Rs), ryanodine receptors (RyRs), the mitochondrial calcium uniporter (MCU), sodium-calcium exchangers (NCX), sarco/endoplasmic reticulum Ca²⁺-ATPase (SERCA), and calcium/calmodulin-dependent protein kinase II (CaMKII).

title: Calcium and TRPML-Mediated Autophagy: Implications in Cancer, Cardiovascular Diseases, and Cardio-Oncology.
chunk_text: Although these regulators are well studied, their disease-specific functions remain context-dependent and complex.

title: Calcium and TRPML-Mediated Autophagy: Implications in Cancer, Cardiovascular Diseases, and Cardio-Oncology.
chunk_text: In cancer, Ca²⁺-regulated autophagy enhances metabolic flexibility, maintains mitochondrial integrity, promotes resistance to chemotherapy, and facilitates immune evasion, thereby supporting tumor growth and survival.

title: Calcium and TRPML-Mediated Autophagy: Implications in Cancer, Cardiovascular Diseases, and Cardio-Oncology.
chunk_text: Conversely, in cardiovascular diseases (CVDs), autophagy enables cardiomyocytes to adapt to ischemic, inflammatory, and hemodynamic stress.

title: Calcium and TRPML-Mediated Autophagy: Implications in Cancer, Cardiovascular Diseases, and Cardio-Oncology.
chunk_text: However, dysregulated Ca²⁺ signaling and impaired autophagic flux contribute to tumor progression and pathological cardiac remodeling, respectively.

title: Calcium and TRPML-Mediated Autophagy: Implications in Cancer, Cardiovascular Diseases, and Cardio-Oncology.
chunk_text: This review explores the molecular mechanisms underlying Ca²⁺-dependent autophagy in cancer and CVDs, providing a detailed analysis of shared signaling pathways and potential therapeutic targets.

title: Calcium and TRPML-Mediated Autophagy: Implications in Cancer, Cardiovascular Diseases, and Cardio-Oncology.
chunk_text: Discussed in this review, the emerging field of cardio-oncology highlights a mechanistic convergence in which anticancer therapies disrupt cardiomyocyte Ca²⁺ homeostasis, causing mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload, ER stress, and defective autophagy, ultimately leading to cardiotoxicity, while tumor cells exploit the same pathways to survive therapeutic stress.

title: Calcium and TRPML-Mediated Autophagy: Implications in Cancer, Cardiovascular Diseases, and Cardio-Oncology.
chunk_text: By elucidating the spatiotemporal dynamics of Ca²⁺ signaling and autophagy, we identify common molecular hubs and propose precision strategies to enhance anticancer efficacy while preserving cardiac function, advancing translational innovation in cardio-oncology.

title: Cardiovascular Risk Reduction With GLP-1 RA Drugs.

title: Cardiovascular Risk Reduction With GLP-1 RA Drugs.
chunk_text: Initially developed as glucose-lowering therapies for type 2 diabetes, these agents have demonstrated broad benefits that extend well beyond glycemic control.

title: Cardiovascular Risk Reduction With GLP-1 RA Drugs.
chunk_text: GLP-1 RAs enhance glucose-dependent insulin secretion and reduce appetite, leading to improved glycemia and sustained weight loss.

title: Cardiovascular Risk Reduction With GLP-1 RA Drugs.
chunk_text: In addition, GLP-1 signaling exerts vascular and myocardial effects, including improved endothelial function, reduced inflammation and oxidative stress, and favorable changes in cardiac metabolism and remodeling.

title: Cardiovascular Risk Reduction With GLP-1 RA Drugs.
chunk_text: Large randomized cardiovascular outcomes trials have consistently shown that several GLP-1 RAs reduce major adverse cardiovascular events, including myocardial infarction, stroke, cardiovascular death, and heart failure.

title: Cardiovascular Risk Reduction With GLP-1 RA Drugs.
chunk_text: Benefits have been observed across diverse populations, including individuals with established cardiovascular disease and those with obesity but without diabetes.

title: Cardiovascular Risk Reduction With GLP-1 RA Drugs.
chunk_text: Emerging therapies targeting multiple incretin pathways, such as dual GIP (glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide)-GLP-1 RAs, may further extend these benefits.

title: Cardiovascular Risk Reduction With GLP-1 RA Drugs.
chunk_text: Collectively, these findings suggest that GLP-1 RAs influence multiple cardiometabolic pathways and may shift the underlying metabolic milieu toward a more favorable physiological state.

title: Cardiovascular Risk Reduction With GLP-1 RA Drugs.
chunk_text: In this Clinical Primer, we review the physiology of GLP-1 signaling, the evidence supporting cardiovascular risk reduction, and practical considerations for the clinical use of incretin-based therapies.

title: Molecular, genetic, and pharmacological advances in type 2 diabetes (2015-2025).

title: Molecular, genetic, and pharmacological advances in type 2 diabetes (2015-2025).

title: Molecular, genetic, and pharmacological advances in type 2 diabetes (2015-2025).

title: Molecular, genetic, and pharmacological advances in type 2 diabetes (2015-2025).
chunk_text: Therapeutically, incretin-based drugs, especially GLP-1 receptor agonists and dual agonists, as well as SGLT2 inhibitors, have transformed outcomes by targeting both glycemic control and cardiovascular-renal protection.

title: Molecular, genetic, and pharmacological advances in type 2 diabetes (2015-2025).

title: Molecular, genetic, and pharmacological advances in type 2 diabetes (2015-2025).
chunk_text: Yet major gaps remain, including functional annotation of risk loci, understanding of β-cell dedifferentiation and recovery, and equitable implementation across diverse populations.

title: Molecular, genetic, and pharmacological advances in type 2 diabetes (2015-2025).
chunk_text: This review synthesizes molecular, genetic, and pharmacological progress from 2015 to 2025, highlights clinical translation, and identifies priorities for the next decade of research.

title: Myo-Inositol in Pregnancy: Implications for Acne Management.

title: PANoptosis in diabetic retinopathy: immunological insights into mechanisms and translational therapies.
chunk_text: Diabetic retinopathy (DR), a leading cause of blindness, is driven by hyperglycemia-induced neurovascular damage.

title: PANoptosis in diabetic retinopathy: immunological insights into mechanisms and translational therapies.
chunk_text: Emerging evidence indicates that PANoptosis, an integrated inflammatory programmed cell death modality encompassing apoptosis, pyroptosis, and necroptosis, participates in the progression of diabetic retinal damage; however, the supporting evidence varies substantially across clinical specimens, diabetic animal models, high-glucose cultured retinal cells, and non-DR inflammatory disease models.

title: PANoptosis in diabetic retinopathy: immunological insights into mechanisms and translational therapies.
chunk_text: This review systematically summarizes the latest advances in PANoptosis-associated mechanisms underlying DR pathogenesis, focusing on PANoptosome signaling networks, non-coding RNA-mediated regulation, and immune-metabolic crosstalk.

title: PANoptosis in diabetic retinopathy: immunological insights into mechanisms and translational therapies.
chunk_text: We outline promising candidate biomarkers including PANoptosis-related gene signatures and inflammatory cell death molecules, and critically evaluate multiple translational therapeutic strategies covering small-molecule inhibitors, gene intervention, and nanomedicine delivery.

title: PANoptosis in diabetic retinopathy: immunological insights into mechanisms and translational therapies.
chunk_text: Importantly, we further address context-dependent dual roles of PANoptosis, potential safety risks of non-selective PANoptosis blockade, challenges in blood-retinal barrier penetration, systemic immune side effects, and pathological heterogeneity among DR subtypes and disease stages.

title: PANoptosis in diabetic retinopathy: immunological insights into mechanisms and translational therapies.
chunk_text: Since PANoptosis also fundamentally contributes to host defense and retinal innate immune homeostasis, DR therapeutic strategies should prioritize biomarker-guided, local ocular delivery, time-dependent stage intervention, and cell-type-specific fine tuning, rather than generalized systemic suppression.

title: PANoptosis in diabetic retinopathy: immunological insights into mechanisms and translational therapies.
chunk_text: By reconciling mechanistic progress with unresolved translational bottlenecks, this review proposes that PANoptosis serves as a dynamic and evolving conceptual framework for interpreting inflammatory neurovascular degeneration in DR, instead of an entirely confirmed clinical therapeutic target.

title: PANoptosis in diabetic retinopathy: immunological insights into mechanisms and translational therapies.
chunk_text: We emphasize the urgent need for DR-specific mechanistic verification, longitudinal biomarker cohort studies, and well-designed multicenter clinical trials to advance safe and precise targeted interventions.

chunk_text: The present review presented a novel barrier‑metabolism‑immunity three‑dimensional regulatory framework that clarifies the core mechanisms of oral‑gut axis dysregulation: Bidirectional translocation of oral and gut microbiota disrupts microbial homeostasis; key metabolites, including short‑chain fatty acids and trimethylamine N‑oxide, mediate interorgan signaling; immune cell migration and barrier damage collectively constitute the pathological basis, which drives multisystem disorders through secondary axes.

title: Controlling Maceration in Diabetic Foot Ulcers: A Scoping Review.
chunk_text: Diabetic foot ulcers (DFUs) are a severe late complication of diabetes, and maceration of the periwound skin is one of the problems that affect the healing process.

title: Controlling Maceration in Diabetic Foot Ulcers: A Scoping Review.
chunk_text: However, no systematic method to control maceration has been established.

title: Controlling Maceration in Diabetic Foot Ulcers: A Scoping Review.
chunk_text: The aim of this scoping review was to summarize the available evidence on management methods that can be used to control maceration in DFUs and to identify gaps in the research.

title: Controlling Maceration in Diabetic Foot Ulcers: A Scoping Review.
chunk_text: Scoping review guided by the PRISMA-Scoping Review (ScR) checklist.

title: Controlling Maceration in Diabetic Foot Ulcers: A Scoping Review.
chunk_text: Four databases (MEDLINE, CINAHL, PubMed, and Google Scholar) were searched using a combination of terms related to the maceration and the DFUs, with 2 independent researchers screening the articles according to the inclusion criteria and then extracting the data.

title: Controlling Maceration in Diabetic Foot Ulcers: A Scoping Review.
chunk_text: The inclusion criteria required that all articles needed to report original research/case studies that involved patients with DFUs, and examined management methods that could be used to control maceration.

title: Controlling Maceration in Diabetic Foot Ulcers: A Scoping Review.
chunk_text: This literature search yielded 1814 articles, of which 16 met the inclusion criteria.

title: Controlling Maceration in Diabetic Foot Ulcers: A Scoping Review.
chunk_text: Three types of management methods were reported for controlling the maceration.

title: Controlling Maceration in Diabetic Foot Ulcers: A Scoping Review.
chunk_text: They were absorption of the exudate (extracted from 10 records), application of antimicrobial (extracted from 5 records), and reducing oxidative stress (extracted from 1 record).

title: Controlling Maceration in Diabetic Foot Ulcers: A Scoping Review.
chunk_text: Although management methods that can be used to control maceration have been examined by previous studies, we found none that investigated which method was the best for treating for the different conditions found for maceration in the DFUs.

title: Controlling Maceration in Diabetic Foot Ulcers: A Scoping Review.
chunk_text: Further research is needed to establish guidelines for selecting the management method in accordance with the severity of the maceration in the DFUs.

title: The effect of sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 inhibitors on haemoglobin and haematocrit in diabetic kidney disease.

title: Real-world outcomes of Finerenone in patients with diabetic kidney disease in Saudi Arabia.

title: Long-Term Health Care Utilization Costs of GLP-1RA Use vs Bariatric Metabolic Surgery.

title: Prevalence and Predictors of Dyslipidemia and Diabetes Mellitus Among cART-Treated HIV Patients in Western Ethiopia: A Cross-Sectional Study.

title: Prevalence and Predictors of Dyslipidemia and Diabetes Mellitus Among cART-Treated HIV Patients in Western Ethiopia: A Cross-Sectional Study.

title: Prevalence and Predictors of Dyslipidemia and Diabetes Mellitus Among cART-Treated HIV Patients in Western Ethiopia: A Cross-Sectional Study.

title: Prevalence and Predictors of Dyslipidemia and Diabetes Mellitus Among cART-Treated HIV Patients in Western Ethiopia: A Cross-Sectional Study.

chunk_text: In addition to commonly recognized risk factors, frequent consumption of a high-fat, carbohydrate-restricted diet like Nitter kibbeh may influence metabolic outcomes in this sub-population, highlighting the need for further research.

title: Prevalence and Predictors of Dyslipidemia and Diabetes Mellitus Among cART-Treated HIV Patients in Western Ethiopia: A Cross-Sectional Study.

title: Prevalence and Predictors of Dyslipidemia and Diabetes Mellitus Among cART-Treated HIV Patients in Western Ethiopia: A Cross-Sectional Study.

title: Bioelectronic modulation of the thymic "genetic mirror": 448 kHz radiofrequency stimulation as a novel strategy for immune tolerance induction in type
chunk_text: Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus (T1DM) arises from a fundamental breakdown of central immune tolerance within the thymus.

title: Bioelectronic modulation of the thymic "genetic mirror": 448 kHz radiofrequency stimulation as a novel strategy for immune tolerance induction in type
chunk_text: Medullary thymic epithelial cells (mTECs) express the Autoimmune Regulator (AIRE) protein, which drives the promiscuous transcription of peripheral tissue-restricted antigens - including insulin - thereby creating a "genetic mirror" essential for the clonal deletion of autoreactive T-cells.

title: Bioelectronic modulation of the thymic "genetic mirror": 448 kHz radiofrequency stimulation as a novel strategy for immune tolerance induction in type
chunk_text: Genetic susceptibility loci that reduce intrathymic insulin expression allow high-affinity insulin-reactive T-cells to escape negative selection and enter the periphery, where they ultimately orchestrate the progressive destruction of pancreatic β-cells.

title: Bioelectronic modulation of the thymic "genetic mirror": 448 kHz radiofrequency stimulation as a novel strategy for immune tolerance induction in type
chunk_text: Despite decades of research, no therapeutic strategy has yet succeeded in restoring this upstream tolerogenic defect at its source.

title: Bioelectronic modulation of the thymic "genetic mirror": 448 kHz radiofrequency stimulation as a novel strategy for immune tolerance induction in type
chunk_text: I hypothesise that the thymic microenvironment can be bioelectronically reprogrammed to re-establish central immune tolerance in T1DM.

title: Bioelectronic modulation of the thymic "genetic mirror": 448 kHz radiofrequency stimulation as a novel strategy for immune tolerance induction in type
chunk_text: Specifically, I propose that non-invasive application of 448 kHz radiofrequency (RF) fields, delivered via a wearable smart patch using subthermal Capacitive-Resistive Electric Transfer (CRET), can selectively upregulate The 448 kHz RF field is hypothesised to interact with L-type voltage-gated calcium channels (Cav1.2/Cav1.3) on mTEC membranes, inducing a controlled, subthermal Ca² This approach represents a conceptual shift from peripheral immunosuppression or symptomatic glycaemic management towards upstream, disease-modifying bioelectronic intervention.

title: Bioelectronic modulation of the thymic "genetic mirror": 448 kHz radiofrequency stimulation as a novel strategy for immune tolerance induction in type

title: Clonal Hematopoiesis and Type 2 Diabetes: A Narrative Review.

title: Clonal Hematopoiesis and Type 2 Diabetes: A Narrative Review.

title: Clonal Hematopoiesis and Type 2 Diabetes: A Narrative Review.

title: Clonal Hematopoiesis and Type 2 Diabetes: A Narrative Review.

title: Clonal Hematopoiesis and Type 2 Diabetes: A Narrative Review.
chunk_text: Preclinical studies found that antidiabetic medication metformin might slow down clonal expansion of CH, while clinical data suggest that anti-inflammatory medications confer better clinical outcomes for certain CH carriers, although their relevance for patients with diabetes remains to be elucidated.

title: The pivotal role of immunometabolism in diabetic neuropathy and its potential therapeutic strategies.
chunk_text: Diabetic peripheral neuropathy (DPN) is a prevalent and severely disabling complication of diabetes mellitus characterized by complex pathophysiological mechanisms.

title: The pivotal role of immunometabolism in diabetic neuropathy and its potential therapeutic strategies.

title: The pivotal role of immunometabolism in diabetic neuropathy and its potential therapeutic strategies.

title: The pivotal role of immunometabolism in diabetic neuropathy and its potential therapeutic strategies.

title: The pivotal role of immunometabolism in diabetic neuropathy and its potential therapeutic strategies.
chunk_text: Furthermore, neuroinflammation in DPN transcends the peripheral nerve-dorsal root ganglion-spinal cord axis, with immune cell activation and inflammatory microenvironment formation directly perpetuating clinical symptoms such as hyperalgesia and hypoesthesia.

title: The pivotal role of immunometabolism in diabetic neuropathy and its potential therapeutic strategies.

title: The pivotal role of immunometabolism in diabetic neuropathy and its potential therapeutic strategies.

title: The pivotal role of immunometabolism in diabetic neuropathy and its potential therapeutic strategies.

title: The pivotal role of immunometabolism in diabetic neuropathy and its potential therapeutic strategies.

title: The pivotal role of immunometabolism in diabetic neuropathy and its potential therapeutic strategies.
chunk_text: Finally, potential therapeutic strategies focusing on immunometabolism are summarized, offering prospects for clinical translation.

title: The pivotal role of immunometabolism in diabetic neuropathy and its potential therapeutic strategies.

title: The pivotal role of immunometabolism in diabetic neuropathy and its potential therapeutic strategies.

title: Religious leaders as partners in diabetes self-care promotion: opportunities and challenges from Imams' perspectives in Saudi Arabia.

chunk_text: 19 Imams were purposively recruited from urban and rural settings and interviewed face-to-face.

chunk_text: Interviews were audio-recorded, and transcribed verbatim in Arabic.

title: Religious leaders as partners in diabetes self-care promotion: opportunities and challenges from Imams' perspectives in Saudi Arabia.
chunk_text: The analysis was performed using inductive thematic analysis to identify key themes related to Imams' perspectives on diabetes self-care promotion.

title: Religious leaders as partners in diabetes self-care promotion: opportunities and challenges from Imams' perspectives in Saudi Arabia.
chunk_text: Five key themes emerged.

title: Religious leaders as partners in diabetes self-care promotion: opportunities and challenges from Imams' perspectives in Saudi Arabia.
chunk_text: First, Imams described their moral authority and community trust as a strong foundation for influencing health-related attitudes and behaviors.

title: Religious leaders as partners in diabetes self-care promotion: opportunities and challenges from Imams' perspectives in Saudi Arabia.
chunk_text: Second, participants identified multiple opportunities to support diabetes self-care through sermons, post-prayer talks, educational sessions, and individual counseling, particularly when health messages were framed within Islamic teachings.

title: Religious leaders as partners in diabetes self-care promotion: opportunities and challenges from Imams' perspectives in Saudi Arabia.
chunk_text: Third, substantial role ambiguity and lack of consensus existed regarding whether diabetes self-care promotion falls within Imams' professional responsibilities.

title: Religious leaders as partners in diabetes self-care promotion: opportunities and challenges from Imams' perspectives in Saudi Arabia.
chunk_text: Fourth, perceived barriers included limited health-related knowledge, concerns about credibility, anticipated congregant resistance, and skepticism about the effectiveness of behavior change efforts.

title: Religious leaders as partners in diabetes self-care promotion: opportunities and challenges from Imams' perspectives in Saudi Arabia.
chunk_text: Finally, participants highlighted the absence of institutional support, formal training, and structured collaboration with healthcare authorities as major constraints, despite general agreement that reinforcing adherence to medical advice aligns with their ethical role.

title: Religious leaders as partners in diabetes self-care promotion: opportunities and challenges from Imams' perspectives in Saudi Arabia.
chunk_text: Imams in Saudi Arabia perceive both meaningful opportunities and significant challenges in engaging as partners in diabetes self-care promotion.

title: Religious leaders as partners in diabetes self-care promotion: opportunities and challenges from Imams' perspectives in Saudi Arabia.
chunk_text: While high levels of trust and social influence position Imams as promising contributors to culturally grounded health initiatives, role ambiguity, training gaps, and lack of institutional support limit their involvement.

title: Religious leaders as partners in diabetes self-care promotion: opportunities and challenges from Imams' perspectives in Saudi Arabia.
chunk_text: Structured training, clear role delineation, and formal collaboration between health and religious institutions may enable effective, faith-aligned strategies to support diabetes self-care and improve health outcomes.

title: Contemporary Concise Review 2025: Asthma.
chunk_text: Asthma research in 2025 further advanced a multidimensional view of asthma, integrating disease trajectory, exacerbation risk, structural airway changes, comorbidities, and treatment responsiveness.

title: Contemporary Concise Review 2025: Asthma.
chunk_text: In mild asthma, studies published in 2025 further reinforced the importance of anti-inflammatory reliever strategies over SABA-only treatment by supporting timely inhaled corticosteroid delivery at symptom worsening to reduce exacerbation risk, particularly in adults and adolescents, while providing emerging but still nuanced evidence in child.

title: Contemporary Concise Review 2025: Asthma.
chunk_text: In severe asthma, the therapeutic focus expanded beyond exacerbation reduction towards disease modification, including oral corticosteroid sparing, improvement or normalisation of lung function, reduction of mucus plugging, and attainment of clinical remission.

title: Contemporary Concise Review 2025: Asthma.
chunk_text: Clinical remission became more clearly defined and increasingly positioned as a treatment target and research endpoint, supported by emerging consensus definitions, real-world data, and analyses of biologic trials.

title: Contemporary Concise Review 2025: Asthma.
chunk_text: Real-world asthma management increasingly emphasised implementation, including treatable-traits-based care, biomarker-informed stratification, digital inhaler technologies, and approaches to persistent ethnic, social, and age-related inequities in outcomes.

title: Characteristics and clinical outcomes of patients with severe asthma receiving tezepelumab after marketing approval in Spain (T-ROSS II study): an obs
chunk_text: Asthma is a prevalent chronic respiratory disease characterised by airway inflammation, reversible airflow obstruction and airway hyper-responsiveness.

title: Characteristics and clinical outcomes of patients with severe asthma receiving tezepelumab after marketing approval in Spain (T-ROSS II study): an obs
chunk_text: Most patients with severe asthma (SA) remain uncontrolled despite the availability of multiple therapies, representing a significant global health burden.Thymic stromal lymphopoietin (TSLP) is an epithelial cell-derived cytokine that plays a central role in asthma initiation via both allergic and non-allergic mechanisms as well as in the persistence of airway inflammation in patients with asthma, acting upstream in the inflammatory cascade.Tezepelumab, a monoclonal antibody that targets TSLP, has shown clinical efficacy and safety in pivotal trials in patients with T2 and non-T2 asthma.

title: Characteristics and clinical outcomes of patients with severe asthma receiving tezepelumab after marketing approval in Spain (T-ROSS II study): an obs
chunk_text: Tezepelumab was approved for use in Europe in October 2022, and the marketing authorisation was issued in Spain in October 2023.

title: Characteristics and clinical outcomes of patients with severe asthma receiving tezepelumab after marketing approval in Spain (T-ROSS II study): an obs
chunk_text: The T-ROSS II study is a retrospective, observational, single-arm, multicentre study planned to include 400 patients aged ≥12 years treated in SA units.

title: Characteristics and clinical outcomes of patients with severe asthma receiving tezepelumab after marketing approval in Spain (T-ROSS II study): an obs
chunk_text: T-ROSS II will represent the largest published series of patients with SA who initiated tezepelumab after its market authorisation.Eligible patients must have at least 12 months of medical history prior to treatment initiation and a minimum of 3 months of follow-up data recorded in the electronic health records.The co-primary outcomes are to describe baseline patient demographics and clinical characteristics and evaluate changes in the annualised asthma exacerbation rate before and after the initiation of tezepelumab.

title: Characteristics and clinical outcomes of patients with severe asthma receiving tezepelumab after marketing approval in Spain (T-ROSS II study): an obs
chunk_text: These findings will complement clinical trial data and inform physicians and healthcare decision-makers about the real-world effectiveness and utilization of tezepelumab in patients with SA.

title: Characteristics and clinical outcomes of patients with severe asthma receiving tezepelumab after marketing approval in Spain (T-ROSS II study): an obs
chunk_text: This study is expected to provide valuable insights into the clinical outcomes and treatment patterns of this population.

chunk_text: The final protocol of the study has been approved by the ethics committee/Institutional Review Board (EC/IRB) of the University Hospital 12 de Octubre (Madrid, Spain) (EC/IRB number: 25/226).

title: Exploratory investigation of changes in blood transcriptome following mepolizumab treatment for asthma.
chunk_text: To evaluate the effects of mepolizumab therapy on the blood transcriptome in patients with severe eosinophilic asthma (SEA) and to determine whether changes in gene expression predict responsiveness to mepolizumab treatment.

title: Exploratory investigation of changes in blood transcriptome following mepolizumab treatment for asthma.
chunk_text: Fourteen consecutive patients diagnosed with SEA were prospectively enrolled.

title: Exploratory investigation of changes in blood transcriptome following mepolizumab treatment for asthma.
chunk_text: Each patient had sequential blood samples obtained pre-initiation and at 4, 8, and 12 weeks post-initiation of mepolizumab.

title: Exploratory investigation of changes in blood transcriptome following mepolizumab treatment for asthma.
chunk_text: Peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) were isolated from blood samples for bulk RNA sequencing.

title: Exploratory investigation of changes in blood transcriptome following mepolizumab treatment for asthma.
chunk_text: Paired differential expression (DE) gene analysis was performed comparing baseline gene expression versus all timepoints.

title: Exploratory investigation of changes in blood transcriptome following mepolizumab treatment for asthma.
chunk_text: Changes in gene expression were also compared between responders versus nonresponders to mepolizumab treatment.

title: Exploratory investigation of changes in blood transcriptome following mepolizumab treatment for asthma.
chunk_text: Finally, gene set enrichment analysis was evaluated across timepoints and its interaction with the response to mepolizumab treatment.

title: Exploratory investigation of changes in blood transcriptome following mepolizumab treatment for asthma.
chunk_text: Principal component analysis of all genes demonstrated a homogeneous distribution, which did not separate into distinct groups based on either timepoint or therapeutic response.

title: Exploratory investigation of changes in blood transcriptome following mepolizumab treatment for asthma.
chunk_text: At week 12, 149 genes had significant DE (FDR < 0.05) compared to baseline.

title: Exploratory investigation of changes in blood transcriptome following mepolizumab treatment for asthma.
chunk_text: When performing all-way timepoint comparisons, 74 genes (63 protein-coding) were found to be downregulated at weeks 4, 8 and 12 compared to baseline, but no differences were observed between post baseline timepoints.

title: Exploratory investigation of changes in blood transcriptome following mepolizumab treatment for asthma.
chunk_text: Twenty-eight genes (16 protein-coding) did change with progression of time and were associated with response to therapy, and 56 protein-coding genes distinguished responders and nonresponders.

title: Exploratory investigation of changes in blood transcriptome following mepolizumab treatment for asthma.
chunk_text: At week 12, nonresponders had a high enrichment of genes involved with signaling pathways, including cytokines, growth factors, cell differentiation markers, and protein kinases.

title: Exploratory investigation of changes in blood transcriptome following mepolizumab treatment for asthma.
chunk_text: In this exploratory study, mepolizumab use was associated with limited global transcriptomic separation with some preliminary differential-expression and enrichment findings.

title: Exploratory investigation of changes in blood transcriptome following mepolizumab treatment for asthma.
chunk_text: No definitive mechanistic or predictive response signatures were identified due to the study limitations.

title: Exploratory investigation of changes in blood transcriptome following mepolizumab treatment for asthma.
chunk_text: Future studies, including larger cohorts followed for longer durations with interrogation of specific immune subsets, may offer additional insight into the pathophysiology of severe eosinophilic asthma and into predicting response to mepolizumab treatment.

title: Harnessing Machine Learning and Electronic Health Record Data to Improve Asthma Management.
chunk_text: The review examines the application of machine learning (ML) and large language models (LLMs) to asthma management.

title: Harnessing Machine Learning and Electronic Health Record Data to Improve Asthma Management.
chunk_text: We sought to identify clinically relevant applications, and particularly those that harness electronic health record data.

title: Harnessing Machine Learning and Electronic Health Record Data to Improve Asthma Management.
chunk_text: We review methodological challenges and future directions for translating these tools into meaningful improvements in asthma care.

title: Harnessing Machine Learning and Electronic Health Record Data to Improve Asthma Management.
chunk_text: ML applied to electronic health record data has been utilized across several domains of asthma management: predicting medication response to inhaled corticosteroids and biologics, improving inhaler adherence through digital inhaler systems, and predicting exacerbation risk with moderate-to-high accuracy.

title: Harnessing Machine Learning and Electronic Health Record Data to Improve Asthma Management.

title: Ethyl ferulate attenuates allergic asthma via inhibiting macrophage M2 polarization through STAT6/IRF4 pathway.

title: Ethyl ferulate attenuates allergic asthma via inhibiting macrophage M2 polarization through STAT6/IRF4 pathway.

title: Ethyl ferulate attenuates allergic asthma via inhibiting macrophage M2 polarization through STAT6/IRF4 pathway.

title: Ethyl ferulate attenuates allergic asthma via inhibiting macrophage M2 polarization through STAT6/IRF4 pathway.
chunk_text: EF alleviates allergic asthma by inhibiting M2 macrophage polarization via disruption of the STAT6/IRF4 signaling pathway, supporting its potential as a therapeutic agent derived from traditional medicine.

title: Biological functions and clinical efficacy of IL-5/IL-5Rα-targeted therapies across eosinophilia-associated diseases.
chunk_text: Interleukin-5 (IL-5) is a central regulator of eosinophil differentiation, maturation, survival, activation, and mobilization, and it contributes to eosinophil recruitment to inflamed tissues.

title: Biological functions and clinical efficacy of IL-5/IL-5Rα-targeted therapies across eosinophilia-associated diseases.
chunk_text: These biological functions have made the IL-5/IL-5 receptor alpha (IL-5Rα) pathway a key therapeutic target in eosinophil-associated diseases.

title: Biological functions and clinical efficacy of IL-5/IL-5Rα-targeted therapies across eosinophilia-associated diseases.
chunk_text: Four biologics currently target this pathway in clinical practice: mepolizumab, reslizumab, and depemokimab bind soluble IL-5, whereas benralizumab targets IL-5Rα and induces antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity.

title: Biological functions and clinical efficacy of IL-5/IL-5Rα-targeted therapies across eosinophilia-associated diseases.
chunk_text: Clinical development has been successful in severe eosinophilic asthma (SEA), where targeting the IL-5/IL-5Rα pathway reduces exacerbation risk and can lower the need for long-term oral corticosteroid use.

title: Biological functions and clinical efficacy of IL-5/IL-5Rα-targeted therapies across eosinophilia-associated diseases.
chunk_text: The therapeutic scope has since expanded to chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps (CRSwNP), eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA), and idiopathic hypereosinophilic syndrome (iHES).

title: Biological functions and clinical efficacy of IL-5/IL-5Rα-targeted therapies across eosinophilia-associated diseases.
chunk_text: Mepolizumab has shown efficacy across these eosinophilia-associated diseases, reducing asthma exacerbations, nasal polyp burden, EGPA relapse activity, HES flares, and eosinophils in peripheral blood.

title: Biological functions and clinical efficacy of IL-5/IL-5Rα-targeted therapies across eosinophilia-associated diseases.
chunk_text: Mepolizumab is approved by both FDA and EMA for SEA, CRSwNP, EGPA, and HES.

title: Biological functions and clinical efficacy of IL-5/IL-5Rα-targeted therapies across eosinophilia-associated diseases.
chunk_text: Reslizumab improves exacerbation rates and lung function in SEA and is approved by FDA and EMA for this indication.

title: Biological functions and clinical efficacy of IL-5/IL-5Rα-targeted therapies across eosinophilia-associated diseases.
chunk_text: Benralizumab produces rapid and near-complete blood and tissue eosinophil depletion, reduces exacerbation rates and oral corticosteroid use in SEA, and has demonstrated sustained remissions and corticosteroid-sparing efficacy in EGPA; it is approved by FDA and EMA for SEA and EGPA.

title: Biological functions and clinical efficacy of IL-5/IL-5Rα-targeted therapies across eosinophilia-associated diseases.

title: Cross-kingdom microbiome interactions along the gut-lung axis: immune-microecological coordination, shared mechanisms, and disease-context dependence
chunk_text: In this Review, we synthesize rapidly expanding evidence that fungi, bacteria, and phages in the gut form an integrated network that may influence susceptibility, inflammatory tone, and therapeutic responsiveness across asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), and lung cancer via the gut-lung axis.

title: Cross-kingdom microbiome interactions along the gut-lung axis: immune-microecological coordination, shared mechanisms, and disease-context dependence
chunk_text: We first summarize how cross-kingdom communities in the intestine are organized and interact, highlighting a tripartite framework centered on pathogen-associated molecular pattern-pattern recognition receptor (PAMP-PRR) circuits, the short-chain fatty acid (SCFA)-regulatory T-cell axis, and tryptophan-indole-aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AHR) signaling.

title: Cross-kingdom microbiome interactions along the gut-lung axis: immune-microecological coordination, shared mechanisms, and disease-context dependence
chunk_text: We then compare how these shared axes are differentially perturbed across asthma, COPD, ARDS, and lung cancer, using these disorders as representative but non-sequential disease contexts along a conceptual gradient of immune-microecological disruption.

title: Cross-kingdom microbiome interactions along the gut-lung axis: immune-microecological coordination, shared mechanisms, and disease-context dependence
chunk_text: Finally, we discuss how dietary modulation, pre-/pro-/postbiotics, mycobiome- and virome-targeted strategies, and phage-based approaches could be rationally combined to restore gut-derived immunometabolic circuits and improve respiratory outcomes.

title: Cross-kingdom microbiome interactions along the gut-lung axis: immune-microecological coordination, shared mechanisms, and disease-context dependence
chunk_text: By integrating cross-kingdom ecology with mucosal immunology, this Review provides an integrative interpretive framework suggesting that gut microbiome-targeted strategies may help refine prevention, stratification, and adjunctive treatment approaches in selected respiratory disease contexts.

title: The Role of Fecal Microbiome Transplantation in Steroid Hyporesponsive Asthma.
chunk_text: Asthma is a chronic inflammatory airway disease characterized by airflow obstruction, airway hyperresponsiveness, and structural remodeling.

title: The Role of Fecal Microbiome Transplantation in Steroid Hyporesponsive Asthma.
chunk_text: Corticosteroids remain the mainstay of asthma therapy; however, a substantial proportion of patients with severe disease develop steroid hyporesponsiveness, limiting therapeutic efficacy and increasing disease burden.

title: The Role of Fecal Microbiome Transplantation in Steroid Hyporesponsive Asthma.
chunk_text: Emerging evidence implicates the gut microbiome as a key regulator of systemic immune responses, with growing relevance to asthma pathogenesis and treatment responsiveness.

title: The Role of Fecal Microbiome Transplantation in Steroid Hyporesponsive Asthma.
chunk_text: In this study, we investigated whether gut microbiota dysbiosis contributes to steroid hyporesponsive lung inflammation and whether fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) can restore steroid responsiveness.

title: The Role of Fecal Microbiome Transplantation in Steroid Hyporesponsive Asthma.
chunk_text: Using a steroid-hyporesponsive asthma model, we demonstrate that the disease is associated with significant gut microbial dysregulation, characterized by reduced microbial diversity and depletion of immunoregulatory taxa.

title: The Role of Fecal Microbiome Transplantation in Steroid Hyporesponsive Asthma.
chunk_text: FMT partially restored gut microbial diversity, normalized community structure, and selectively replenished beneficial commensal bacteria, including Akkermansia muciniphila and Faecalibacterium prausnitzii, while suppressing pathogenic taxa.

title: The Role of Fecal Microbiome Transplantation in Steroid Hyporesponsive Asthma.
chunk_text: Importantly, restoration of gut microbial balance was associated with attenuation of lung inflammation and improved steroid responsiveness.

title: The Role of Fecal Microbiome Transplantation in Steroid Hyporesponsive Asthma.
chunk_text: These findings support a functional gut-lung axis in steroid hyporesponsive asthma and identify modulation of gut microbiota as a potential therapeutic strategy.

title: The Role of Fecal Microbiome Transplantation in Steroid Hyporesponsive Asthma.

title: Th17/Treg imbalance: a key driver of neutrophilic inflammation in severe asthma.

title: Th17/Treg imbalance: a key driver of neutrophilic inflammation in severe asthma.

title: Peak Expiratory flow Rate for Emergency Department Management of Acute Asthma Exacerbation: A Randomized Controlled Trial Pilot Study.
chunk_text: Patients were enrolled within 15 min of their first nebulizer treatment.

title: Peak Expiratory flow Rate for Emergency Department Management of Acute Asthma Exacerbation: A Randomized Controlled Trial Pilot Study.
chunk_text: PEFR was measured at baseline and every 30 min until symptom resolution, discharge, or admission.

title: Peak Expiratory flow Rate for Emergency Department Management of Acute Asthma Exacerbation: A Randomized Controlled Trial Pilot Study.
chunk_text: Clinicians in the PEFR-guided group received PEFR values with guideline-based recommendations, while clinicians in the non-PEFR-guided group were blinded to PEFR values.

title: Peak Expiratory flow Rate for Emergency Department Management of Acute Asthma Exacerbation: A Randomized Controlled Trial Pilot Study.
chunk_text: The primary outcome was the proportion of patients with none or mild asthma symptoms by 150 min after enrollment.

title: Peak Expiratory flow Rate for Emergency Department Management of Acute Asthma Exacerbation: A Randomized Controlled Trial Pilot Study.
chunk_text: The secondary outcome was hospitalization.

title: Peak Expiratory flow Rate for Emergency Department Management of Acute Asthma Exacerbation: A Randomized Controlled Trial Pilot Study.
chunk_text: A total of 210 patients were enrolled (101 PEFR-guided; 109 non-PEFR-guided).

title: Peak Expiratory flow Rate for Emergency Department Management of Acute Asthma Exacerbation: A Randomized Controlled Trial Pilot Study.
chunk_text: Symptom control by 150 min occurred in 73 (72%) vs. 78 (72%) patients (95% CI, -11% to 13%).

chunk_text: Hospitalization was similar: 17 (17%) in the PEFR group vs. 13 (12%) in the non-PEFR group (95% CI, -5% to 14%).

chunk_text: Subgroup hospitalization results by percent-predicted PEFR were comparable.

title: Peak Expiratory flow Rate for Emergency Department Management of Acute Asthma Exacerbation: A Randomized Controlled Trial Pilot Study.
chunk_text: In this randomized trial, PEFR-guided care did not improve symptom control, reduce resource utilization, or affect disposition compared with symptom-guided care.

title: Peak Expiratory flow Rate for Emergency Department Management of Acute Asthma Exacerbation: A Randomized Controlled Trial Pilot Study.
chunk_text: These findings suggest limited benefit of PEFR use in ED asthma management.

title: Peak Expiratory flow Rate for Emergency Department Management of Acute Asthma Exacerbation: A Randomized Controlled Trial Pilot Study.

title: Association of Inhaled Corticosteroid and Long-Acting Beta Agonist Combination Versus Inhaled Corticosteroid Only Inhalers on Adherence and Asthma Out
chunk_text: Despite asthma being one of the most common chronic conditions among children in the United States, real-world evidence on medication adherence and healthcare utilization in underserved pediatric populations remains limited.

title: Association of Inhaled Corticosteroid and Long-Acting Beta Agonist Combination Versus Inhaled Corticosteroid Only Inhalers on Adherence and Asthma Out
chunk_text: Therefore, we aim to evaluate adherence, persistence, and asthma-related healthcare utilization among Medicaid-enrolled children receiving inhaled corticosteroids (ICS) versus ICS-long-acting beta-agonist (ICS-LABA) inhalers.

title: Association of Inhaled Corticosteroid and Long-Acting Beta Agonist Combination Versus Inhaled Corticosteroid Only Inhalers on Adherence and Asthma Out
chunk_text: We conducted a retrospective cohort study using Texas Children's Health Plan claims data from 2016 to 2019.

title: Association of Inhaled Corticosteroid and Long-Acting Beta Agonist Combination Versus Inhaled Corticosteroid Only Inhalers on Adherence and Asthma Out
chunk_text: Children aged 4-17 years with asthma diagnoses and ≥1 prescription for an ICS or ICS-LABA were included.

title: Association of Inhaled Corticosteroid and Long-Acting Beta Agonist Combination Versus Inhaled Corticosteroid Only Inhalers on Adherence and Asthma Out
chunk_text: Adherence was determined as the proportion of days covered (PDC) of ≥ 50%, and persistence was defined as continuous therapy without a 60-day gap.

title: Association of Inhaled Corticosteroid and Long-Acting Beta Agonist Combination Versus Inhaled Corticosteroid Only Inhalers on Adherence and Asthma Out
chunk_text: The PDC ≥ 80% was evaluated in sensitivity analysis.

title: Association of Inhaled Corticosteroid and Long-Acting Beta Agonist Combination Versus Inhaled Corticosteroid Only Inhalers on Adherence and Asthma Out
chunk_text: Multivariable logistic regression was used to estimate adherence, asthma-related emergency department (ED) visits, and hospitalizations.

title: Association of Inhaled Corticosteroid and Long-Acting Beta Agonist Combination Versus Inhaled Corticosteroid Only Inhalers on Adherence and Asthma Out
chunk_text: Among 8622 eligible children, 82.4% received ICS and 17.6% received ICS-LABA.

title: Association of Inhaled Corticosteroid and Long-Acting Beta Agonist Combination Versus Inhaled Corticosteroid Only Inhalers on Adherence and Asthma Out
chunk_text: In inverse probability of treatment weighting (IPTW) adjusted analyses, compared with ICS, ICS-LABA users had higher adherence rate i.e., 21.08% versus 16.56% achieved PDC ≥ 50% (aOR = 1.35; 95%CI: 1.25-1.45), and 10.82% versus 6.74% achieved PDC ≥ 80% (aOR = 1.61; 95%CI: 1.31-1.97).

title: Association of Inhaled Corticosteroid and Long-Acting Beta Agonist Combination Versus Inhaled Corticosteroid Only Inhalers on Adherence and Asthma Out
chunk_text: Refill persistence was also higher with ICS-LABA (36.16% vs. 23.35%, aOR: 1.86, 95%CI: 1.74-1.99).

title: Association of Inhaled Corticosteroid and Long-Acting Beta Agonist Combination Versus Inhaled Corticosteroid Only Inhalers on Adherence and Asthma Out
chunk_text: However, despite better adherence, we found ICS-LABA users exhibited greater asthma-related healthcare utilization, i.e., ED visits occurred in 18.69% versus 11.25% (aOR = 1.81; 95%CI: 1.56-2.11) and hospitalizations in 2.53% versus 1.00% (aOR= 2.55; 95%CI: 1.68-3.89), which persisted even after adjusting for adherence.

title: Association of Inhaled Corticosteroid and Long-Acting Beta Agonist Combination Versus Inhaled Corticosteroid Only Inhalers on Adherence and Asthma Out
chunk_text: We found that adherence was greater in children with asthma who were dispensed an ICS-LABA as compared to those dispensed an ICS-only inhaler; however, adherence rates were low in both groups.

title: Association of Inhaled Corticosteroid and Long-Acting Beta Agonist Combination Versus Inhaled Corticosteroid Only Inhalers on Adherence and Asthma Out
chunk_text: Higher hospitalization and ED visit rates among those prescribed ICS-LABA therapy likely reflect higher disease severity in this group.

title: Association of Inhaled Corticosteroid and Long-Acting Beta Agonist Combination Versus Inhaled Corticosteroid Only Inhalers on Adherence and Asthma Out
chunk_text: Our findings should be interpreted in light of claims-based adherence measures and potential residual confounding due to unmeasured asthma severity and control.
